coredata.dc:title: Microenvironment-activatable cascaded responsive carbonized polymer dots as a theranostic platform for precise rapamycin delivery to potentiate the synergy of chemotherapy and γδ T cells-mediated immunotherapy against tumor
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hua
Zhao, Haijie
Huang, Yan
Sun, Guodong
Zhang, Yi
coredata.dc:description: The combination of chemotherapy and immunotherapy (also known as chemo/immunotherapy) promoted by nanomedicine has the potential to enhance the therapeutic effect in more patient populations. However, it is still affected by the inactivation of immune cells induced by traditional chemotherapy, which greatly impairs the synergistic effect of chemotherapy/immunotherapy substantially. Moreover, once tumor cells lose their MHC expression, the current popular CD8+ T cell-based immunotherapy may fail consequently. Herein, rapamycin, as mTOR signaling inhibitor associated with serious side effect once systemically administered, was incorporated into shell-core theranostic nanoplatform to augment synergy of chemotherapy and γδ T cell-mediated MHC-unrestricted immunotherapy against tumor without toxicity on γδ T cells. This nano-platform assembled from a cleavable shell and a carbonized polymer dot core can achieve accurate delivery of rapamycin with traceable imaging and cascade response. Such cascaded responsiveness has endowed it with improved blood compatibility, effective tumor accumulation, microenvironment-selective endocytosis, and hierarchical drug release facilitated by microenvironment-triggered programmable properties (size shrinkage, charge conversion and degradability) transition responding to blood-tissue-cells multi-level biological barriers. In vitro and in vivo experiments have shown that the nanoplatform not only promotes tumor chemotherapy by down-regulating mTOR signals, but also enhances γδ T cell-mediated immunotherapy by promoting the NKG2D pathway in a microenvironment-selective manner. In addition, the TCR-δ knockout mouse model was used to verify the synergy of chemical/immunotherapy promoted by the nanoplatform. In addition to multifunctional integrated therapeutic and diagnostic strategies, we also provide a paradigm to enhance the synergistic effect of chemotherapy/immunotherapy, and broaden the horizons of immunotherapy through γδ T cell-mediated replacement therapy assisted by nano-drugs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa formation procedure–(A) synthetic process of disulfide-bond-bearing hyperbranched poly(amido amine), (B) synthetic process of charge-reversible mPEG-PEI-DMMA, (C) formation route of PPD@ HPAP-CDs/Rapa.
Schematic of the imaging-trackable, cascade-responsive PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa theranostic platform to exert rapamycin-facilitated synergy of chemotherapy and γδ T cells-mediated immunotherapy.

Physicochemical and bioimaging property of HPAP-CPDs. (A) Ultraviolet-visible spectrum of HPAP and HPAP-CPD, the inset illustrates the photoes of samples solution excitated by UV light at 365 nm. (B) Fluorescence emission spectrum of HPAP-CPDs excitated by different wavelengths. (C) TEM images of HPAP-CPDs and its partial enlarged detail (right). (D) Laser confocal microscopic images (excited by 405, 488, and 543 nm) of HPAP-CPDs-treated 4T1 tumor cells, scale bar represents 50 μm.

The effect of tumor microenvironment-triggered size/potential alterations of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa on endocytosis, lysosomes escape, and drug release–(A) TEM morphology of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa complexes. Zeta potential (B) and particle size (C) of HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, and PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa incubated at different pH. (D) Fluorescent histograms of 4T1 cells treated with PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa at pH 6.8 and 7.4 assessed by flow cytometry, the quantified endocytosis rate was presented in the inset. (E) Microscopic fluorescence co-localization images of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa-treated 4T1 cells. HPAP-CDs were stained with FITC (green), while lysosome was stained with Alexa Fluor 594 (red). Scale bars–10 μm. (F) Rapamycin release profiles under different pH and GSH condition.

Biocompatible evaluations of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa–(A) Hemolysis rate of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa and HPAP-CPDs/Rapa along the concentration. (B) Serum stability of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa and HPAP-CPDs/Rapa in 10% FBS determined by size variation along the time. (C) Cytotoxicity of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa and HPAP-CPDs/Rapa against HUVECs. (D) Cell viability of γδ T cells treated with HPAP-CPDs, PPD@HPAP-CPDs, and PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa at pH 7.4. n = 3, * p ≤ 0.05, ** p ≤ 0.01, *** p ≤ 0.001.

PPD@HPAP-CDs/Rapa significantly promoted apoptosis and inhibited proliferation of tumor cells in vitro (pH 6.8)–(A) Schematic for apoptosis and proliferation of tumor cell regulated by mTOR signaling pathway. (B) Tumor cells treated with various formulations (PBS, rapamycin, PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, and PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa) (concentration of rapamycin was 100 nM) for 24 h at pH 7.4 or pH 6.8, the quantity of tumor cells were counted for 4 days. n = 3. (C&D) Tumor cells treated with various formulations (PBS, rapamycin, PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa and PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa) (concentration of rapamycin was 100 nM) for 24 h at pH 7.4 or pH 6.8, the apoptosis of tumor cells were assessed by flow cytometry (C) and the phosphorylation of S6 were assessed by Western Blot (D), n = 3. * p ≤ 0.05, ** p ≤ 0.01, *** p ≤ 0.001.

PPD@HPAP-CDs/Rapa significantly promoted anti-tumor immunity mediated by γδ T cells–(A) The STAT5 signaling regulated by rapamycin in γδ T cells. (B) Various formulations-treated γδ T cells (pH 6.8 or pH 7.4) were co-culture with 4T1 tumor cells (E:T = 5:1) to determine the anti-tumor immune effect, n = 3. (C) γδ T cells treated with different formulations (control, rapamycin, PPS@HPAP-CDs/Rapa and PPD@HPAP-CDs/Rapa) (concentration of rapamycin was 100 nM) for 24 h at pH 7.4 or pH 6.8, then stimulated with ionomycin (1 μg/mL) and PMA (50 ng/mL) for 6 h and labeled for TNF-α and NKG2D. 3 independent experiments were performed in statistical analysis of flow cytometry. (D) γδ T cells treated with various formulations (PBS, rapamycin, PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, and PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa) (concentration of rapamycin was 100 nM) for 24 h in pH 6.8, and the phosphorylation of STAT5 were assessed by Western Blot, n = 3. * p ≤ 0.05, ** p ≤ 0.01, *** p ≤ 0.001.

Organ accumulation and damage of various formulations (A) Ex vivo fluorescence images of tumor and main organs from tumor-bearing mouse treated with various formulations at 14th day (The various formulations were intravenous injected every other day). (B) Images of tissue sections stained with H&E after tumor-bearing mouse treated with various formulations for 14 days. Scale bar–100 μm.

PPD@HPAP-CDs/Rapa significantly inhibited tumor development and promoted in vivo anti-tumor immunity mediated by γδ T cells. (A) A total of 36 sex-and age-matched B6 wt mice were injected s.c. with 5 × 105 cells on day 0, and then randomly divided into 4 groups and treated with various formulations (PBS, rapamycin, PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa and PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa) (concentration of rapamycin was 1.5 mg/kg) every other day respectively, and growth was recorded every other day. Tumor size ≥ 4 × 4 mm was considered positive, n = 3. (B) Images of tumors sacrificed from tumor-bearing mouse treated with various formulations for 14 days. (C-E) Images of tumor tissue sections stained with H&E (C) (Scale bar–50 μm.), Ki67 (D) (Scale bar–100 μm.) and TUNEL (E) (Scale bar–100 μm.) after tumor-bearing mouse treated with various formulations for 14 days. Scale bar–50 μm. (F) Isolated lymphocyte from tumor tissues, which were from mouse bearing tumor and treated with various formulations for 14 days, FACS analysis the rate of NKG2D in γδ T cells. n = 9. * p ≤ 0.05, ** p ≤ 0.01, *** p ≤ 0.001.

PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa-treated improved outcome in adoptive therapy. (A) TCR-δ KO mouse, defines a mouse model in which the target is permanently inactivated in the whole animal. (B) Schematic representation of γδ T cells and PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa treatment protocol. (C) Tumor growth curves of four groups mouse were recorded every other day. (D) Photographs of representative tumor after reconstructed with γδ T cells or PBS, and treated with PPD@HPAP-CDs/Rapa or PBS. n = 9. * p ≤ 0.05, ** p ≤ 0.01, *** p ≤ 0.001.

head.ce:title: Microenvironment-activatable cascaded responsive carbonized polymer dots as a theranostic platform for precise rapamycin delivery to potentiate the synergy of chemotherapy and γδ T cells-mediated immunotherapy against tumor

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The combination of chemotherapy and immunotherapy (also known as chemo/immunotherapy) promoted by nanomedicine has the potential to enhance the therapeutic effect in more patient populations. However, it is still affected by the inactivation of immune cells induced by traditional chemotherapy, which greatly impairs the synergistic effect of chemotherapy/immunotherapy substantially. Moreover, once tumor cells lose their MHC expression, the current popular CD8+ T cell-based immunotherapy may fail consequently. Herein, rapamycin, as mTOR signaling inhibitor associated with serious side effect once systemically administered, was incorporated into shell-core theranostic nanoplatform to augment synergy of chemotherapy and γδ T cell-mediated MHC-unrestricted immunotherapy against tumor without toxicity on γδ T cells. This nano-platform assembled from a cleavable shell and a carbonized polymer dot core can achieve accurate delivery of rapamycin with traceable imaging and cascade response. Such cascaded responsiveness has endowed it with improved blood compatibility, effective tumor accumulation, microenvironment-selective endocytosis, and hierarchical drug release facilitated by microenvironment-triggered programmable properties (size shrinkage, charge conversion and degradability) transition responding to blood-tissue-cells multi-level biological barriers. In vitro and in vivo experiments have shown that the nanoplatform not only promotes tumor chemotherapy by down-regulating mTOR signals, but also enhances γδ T cell-mediated immunotherapy by promoting the NKG2D pathway in a microenvironment-selective manner. In addition, the TCR-δ knockout mouse model was used to verify the synergy of chemical/immunotherapy promoted by the nanoplatform. In addition to multifunctional integrated therapeutic and diagnostic strategies, we also provide a paradigm to enhance the synergistic effect of chemotherapy/immunotherapy, and broaden the horizons of immunotherapy through γδ T cell-mediated replacement therapy assisted by nano-drugs.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is still the main cause of human deaths worldwide. Cancer immunotherapy is now revolutionizing the field of oncology by rejuvenating the immune system to extend the survival of patients with rapidly fatal cancers. Current main immunotherapies including immune checkpoint blockade therapy [1], adoptive cell therapy using engineered T cells expressing chimeric antigen receptors [2], cancer vaccines and cytokine therapy mainly depended on CD4+/CD8+ T cells. Once cancer cells lose their major histocompatibility complex (MHC) expression, they cannot be recognized by conventional CD4+/CD8+ T cells by an antigen-specific pattern, resulting in a low percentage of tumor-infiltrating T cells, which lead to enhancing resistance to immunotherapy.

Among the immune cell options, the γδ T cell, as a subgroup of T cells with distinct γ and δ chains on the surface, has emerged as a new-found promising candidate for tumor therapy [3]. γδ T cells exhibit unique functions which bridge the gap between innate and adaptive immunity, and contributing to lymphoid antitumor surveillance. Moreover, γδ T cells play an important role in early anti-tumor immunity, where they rapidly recognize endogenous stress-induced ligands or exogenous pathogens in a MHC-unrestricted manner and subsequently evoke adaptive immunity [4]. Activated γδ T cells display various effector functions, including antigen-presenting functions, regulatory abilities, cytokine and chemokine production, and cytotoxicity against infected or tumor cells [5], thus they are involving in a series of diseases, including cancer, infection and autoimmunity [6] , [7]. Recent reports describe that γδ T cells exert cytotoxicity on tumor cells through the natural killer group 2 member D (NKG2D) pathway [8,9]. NKG2D encoded by Klrk1 is highly expressed on a subpopulation of γδ T cells and NK cells. In addition, its ligands are highly expressed on compressed target cells, such as infection or oncogenic transformation [10]. Therefore, NKG2D [11,12] have been revealed to mediate the activation of tumors by immune cells.

Recently, it was reported that the antitumor immunity of γδ T cells could be promoted by rapamycin via suppressing mTOR signaling and then up-regulated NKG2D [13]. Rapamycin, the first anticancer agent targeting mTOR was initially reported in 1990s [14]. Through inhibiting mTOR, it blocks tumor cell growth, proliferation, and survival. Therefore, rapamycin is an ideal candidate to exert both chemotherapeutic anticancer effect and γδ T cell-mediated antitumor immunity. Nevertheless, the application of rapamycin in clinical is associated with many systemic side effects, such as gastrointestinal disorders, thrombocytopenia, hyperlipidemia, and diabetes.

Individually, chemotherapy is hampered by dose-limiting toxicity, while immunotherapy demonstrates a low response rates in patients [15]. Therefore, the combined use of immunotherapy and chemotherapy (called chemo/immunotherapy) may complement each other and enhance the treatment effect for more patient groups. Particularly, the emerging delivery systems provide platforms for combined chemo/immunotherapy, facilitating targeted delivery of drugs and immunomodulators as well as reducing systemic side effects [16]. However, there is a longstanding barrier in the chemo/immuno combination therapy: most chemotherapeutic drugs kill tumor cells as well as inhibit immune cells (T cells, NK cells and dendritic cells) at tumor site [17], which substantially compromises the performance of antitumor immune response, causing a chemotherapy-induced immunosuppression. Although it was reported that several chemotherapeutic drugs (e.g., oxaliplatin, doxorubicin and paclitaxel) could induce immunogenic tumor cell death that regress the tumors cells meanwhile secrete distress signals to further promote systemic tumor-specific immune responses [18] , [19]. Although in some case, low-dose chemotherapeutic drugs may reduce immunosuppressive cells thereby improving the efficacy of immunotherapy, while normal doses of chemotherapeutic drugs can kill immune cells at best, this has not been excluded. Thus, it still a challenge to overcome current contradiction between immunotherapy and chemotherapy for augment of synergistic chemo/immunotherapy therapeutic effects substantially.

Currently, the nanomedicines-based advanced drug delivery systems burgeon towards two exciting directions currently. One is the cascaded responsiveness: the presence of multi-stage biological barriers along the delivery route require the delivery carriers to possess multiple responsive property (e.g., synchronous switch of surface charge, carrier size, and assembly stability) adapted to biological barriers switch from blood to tumor microenvironment, to maximize delivery efficiency at the targeted site [20]. Another direction is the visualization of delivery systems: by combining nanomedicine with imaging functionalities (optical, magnetic, and radioactive modalities), it may be beneficial for tracing drug carrier distribution and positioning tumor site in the image-guided combination therapy [21]. Among the theranostic nanoplatform for nanomedicine, carbon dots with excellent fluorescent property exhibit biocompatibility, light-stability, permeability, low cost, and ease of preparation, setting up solid base for in vivo bioimaging and theranostics [22]. Recently, a new emerging form of carbon dots, carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) with a unique polymer/carbon hybrid structure, has been developed and shown extra property inherited from the polymer other than prominent optical property [23]. The reserved polymer chains on CPDs contribute to the multi-functionalization for theranostic application. In combination of the two directions, an integrated delivery vehicle with cascaded response capabilities to overcome multi-stage biological barriers and simultaneous imaging tracking will be appealing.

In addition to imaging-trackable tumor-selective rapamycin delivery capabilities, the therapeutic diagnostic platform also uses rapamycin-based co-chemotherapy/ immunotherapy (scheme 2) to inhibit tumor cell proliferation by down-regulating mTOR signals while enhancing γδ T cell-mediated antitumor immunity with NKG2D by elevating signal transducers and activators of transcription 5 (STAT5) signaling. More importantly, this rapamycin-loaded nanoplatform showed no cytotoxicity on γδ T cells that overcome the longstanding contradiction of traditional chemotherapeutic drugs-induced non-specific cytotoxic effect on immune cells. In addition to the popular immunotherapy strategy based on CD8+ T cells, we propose an alternative method based on γδ T cells, which is promoted by nanomedicine strategies and broadens the methods of tumor immunotherapy.The synergy of chemotherapy and immunotherapy was verified through in vitro and in vivo studies, as well as TCR-δ knock-out (KO) mouse model with deficient γδ T-cell receptor expression. In summary, we combine the nanomedicine platform of imaging traceability, cascade reaction, and therapeutic diagnosis with the synergistic effect of rapamycin-based chemotherapy and γδ T cell-mediated immunotherapy, which may enhance chemotherapy/immunity synergistic effect and broaden the horizon of immunotherapy with nanomedicine-assisted γδ T cells-mediated alternative.

Herein, the carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) prepared by disulfide-bond-containing hyperbranched poly(amido amine) end-capped with polyethylenimine (600 Da) (HPAP) as a precursor was introduced for fluorescence-trackable drug delivery based on following considerations. Firstly, carbon dots (CDs) was developed as hybrid nanomaterials dominated by zero-dimensional carbon, with a size less than 20 nm [25], providing a huge specific surface area for drug adsorption. Secondly, the positive charged HPAPs retained around the CDs allows cationic CPDs to undergo endocytosis and transcytosis to augments tumor penetration [26], while the presence of disulfide bonds in HPAPs aid to intracellular degradation that facilitates drug release. Thirdly, HPAPs passivated, N-doped CDs render predominant fluorescence quantum yield [24], contributing to bioimaging.

For HPAP synthesis, Michael addition polymerization with one-pot two-step was introduced (Scheme 1A). Besides, the HPAP-decorated CPDs (HPAP-CPDs) were prepared by facile and efficient microwave treatment for only 5 min. The chemical structure of HPAP and HPAP-CPDs was confirmed by 1H NMR (Figure S1), it was found that the HPAP-CPDs existed the characteristic peak of HPAP, implying the formation of HPAP/carbon hybrid structure for HPAP-CPDs. Moreover, dynamic light scattering (DLS), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), and gel permeation chromatography (GPC) were utilized to characterize the physicochemical property of HPAP-CPDs. DLS displays the hydrodynamic diameter of HPAP-CPDs about 34.3 nm in the water(Table S1), while the particle size in dry state under HRTEM was 6–10 nm with 0.2 nm lattice spacing (Fig. 1 C). The size difference may attribute to the varying state of HPAP around the carbon core from stretched chain in the water to shrinkage after drying. In addition, the Zeta potential declines from 30.3 mV for HPAP to 20.5 mV for HPAP-CPDs (Table S1) because of the loss of amino groups during the microwave-assisted pyrolysis. GPC was employed to determine the glutathione-triggered degradation of HPAP-CPDs. As shown in Figure S3, two peaks (7.05 and 7.88) with longer elution time appeared after glutathione treatment compared to the original peak at 6.22 of HPAP-CPDs. It indicated that the molecular weight reduction and wider distribution were induced by the degradation of HPAP chain around the carbon main body to irregular segments. Above results demonstrated that HPAP-CPDs were successfully prepared. What's more, these results indicated that HPAP-CPDs not only possessed the small size as traditional CDs, but also inherited the versatile properties from HPAP, involving positively charged surface and glutathione-responsive biodegradability.

The optical performance of HPAP-CPDs was verified by visual observation, Ultraviolet-visible and fluorescence spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 1A&B. As expected, the HPAP-CPDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence compared with HPAP under the irradiation of UV lamp at 365 nm (inset in Fig. 1A), further demonstrating the successful preparation of fluorescent CPDs by microwave-assisted approach. The UV–vis spectra (Fig. 1A) provides structural information that an additional absorption peak at 360 nm emerges in the group of HPAP-CPDs in comparison to HPAP and is belong to n–π* transition from chromophores with lone pairs electron [27]. Besides, fluorescence spectra in Fig. 1B shows that HPAP-CPDs possess the excitation-dependent fluorescence emission. The maximum emission peak (440 nm) appears under 360 nm excitation and emission intensity attenuated gradually along with the excitation wavelength increases from 360 to 600 nm, as well as right shifting of emission wavelength from 440 to 610 nm. This wide emission wavelength distribution is attributed to the CPDs contain a variety of fluorophore compositions in different chemical environment as a fluorescent cocktail [28]. To prove the bioimaging ability of HPAP-CPDs, cell imaging of HPAP-CPDs-treated 4T1 tumor cells was conducted under laser scanning confocal microscopy at different excitation wavelengths. Fig. 1D depicts that HPAP-CPDs-treated cells possess strong blue (under 405 nm excitation) and moderate green (under 488 nm excitation) fluorescence signal, but almost invisible red fluorescence excited at 543 nm. The above cell imaging also showed an excitation-dependent manner that was consistent with the fluorescence spectra result. It manifested that HPAP-CPDs could be endocytosed by cells and showed the potential for cellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa and tumor microenvironment-triggered cascaded response and drug release
ce:section.ce:para: To shield positive charge on HPAP-CPDs during blood circulation and endow with tumor-targeting, HPAP-CPDs/Rapa was formed by loading rapamycin onto HPAP-CPDs firstly and then electrostatic assembled with mPEG-PEI-DMMA (PPD) outer layer that showed pH-triggered charge-conversion. The final obtained core-shell structure was denoted as PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa. Chemical characterization of PPD shell layer by 1H NMR was presented as Fig. S2. Drug loading capacity determined by HPLC was calculated to be 31.9%, which was considerably high benefiting from high specific surface area of CPDs and π-π stacking between conjugated π-domains within both CPDs and rapamycin. Moreover, the encapsulation by PEGylated layer may also contribute to the high loading capacity. As presented in Table S1, the PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa displayed much larger size (220.4 nm) measured by DLS than HPAP-CPDs and negatively charged (−15.9 mV) owing to the shielding effect of PEGylated shell layer. In addition, the HRTEM further provided the morphology of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa as shown in Fig. 2 A. It was found that PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa was a polymer-packaged spherical nanoparticle containing CPDs clusters, just like “cluster bomb”, in which small-sized rapamycin-loaded CPDs looked like “bomblets” encapsulated in the PEGylated shielding “pocket”. This structure was designed not only to eliminate the nonspecific interaction effectively in circulation by PEGylated shielding, but also was able to fire “CPDs bomblets” under the microenvironment-driven cleavage of shielding “pocket”.

Hence, it further verified that the acidic tumor microenvironment triggered the rupture of the "cluster bomb" to achieve simultaneous changes in charge and size. In our design, the acidity-responsive charge-convertible dimethylmaleic acid (DMMA) modification on polycation was introduced to the PEGylated shielding shell (mPEG-PEI-DMMA), which could undergo DMMA hydrolysis and re-expose positive charged amino groups on mPEG-PEI sensitively responding to the acidic microenvironment (i.e., tumor issue) [29], leading to PEGylated shell detachment from the positively charged core of HPAP-CPDs clusters driven by electrostatic repulsion. In this case, drug-loaded HPAP-CPDs with a smaller size and positive charge are released from the ruptured pegylated shielding "pocket", thereby simultaneous charge conversion and size shrinkage, which is expected to enhance tumor penetration and specific accumulation[30].

As shown in Fig. 2B&C, the size shrinkage and charge conversion triggered by pH value were measured on a Malvern Zetasizer at both pH 6.8 (tumor extracellular microenvironment pH) and pH 7.4 (physiological pH). In the experiment, succinic anhydride (SA)-modified mPEG-PEI (PPS) was employed as the control group, which was stable under the acidic microenvironment without SA hydrolysis to cause charge conversion. In addition, the molecular formula and 1H NMR of PPS were also presented in Figure S2. In comparison to the PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa with similar negative zeta potential at both pH 6.8 and 7.4, PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa transformed its zeta potential from −15.9 mV (pH 7.4) to 11.3 mV (pH 6.8), suggesting charge conversion. As expected, the particle size of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa shrank by more than a half from 220.4 nm at pH 7.4 to 97.8 nm at pH 6.8, whereas the PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa revealed no significant change in size. It implied that PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa would be likely to exhibit synchronous size shrinkage and charge conversion from blood to tumor extracellular microenvironment.

Besides, the impact of pH-triggered size shrinkage and charge conversion on cellular uptake was further assessed. The flow cytometry histograms in Fig. 2D demonstrates that PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa treated tumor cells cultured at pH 6.8 displays much higher fluorescence over cultured at pH 7.4, indicating the significantly enhanced endocytosis rate is found at pH 6.8 in comparison to pH 7.4 (inset in Fig. 2D). While PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa at pH 7.4 is comparable to control group. The result confirmed that the pH-triggered charge conversion and size shrinkage at pH 6.8 benefited to the endocytosis due to rupture of the PPD shielding shell exposing to acidic pH and the released smaller-sized CPDs with positive charge inclined to stronger electrostatic interaction with negatively charged cell membrane [31]. Thus, such pH-selective endocytosis based on charge and size transition of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa is believed to improve the tumor targeting and reduce side effects to normal tissue. After endocytosis, the lysosome escape of HPAP-CPDs/Rapa was also evaluated by co-localization of the CPDs and lysosomes under LSCM. To exert therapeutic effect, drugs should escape from lysosomes for avoiding inactivation or excretion. In Fig. 2E, HPAP-CPDs/Rapa complexes (green fluorescence) distribute all over the cytoplasm after 6 h incubation and a portion of them co-localized with the lysotracker (red fluorescence) to show overlay signals with yellow fluorescence. Whereas other portion of green fluorescence appears separated with red fluorescence, suggesting the major HPAP-CPDs/Rapa complexes escaped from lysosomes owing to the “proton sponge effect” of HPAP around CPDs core [32]. The HPAP-CPDs/Rapa complexes that can effectively be escaped from lysosomes may facilitate efficient chemotherapy.

In regard to cascaded-responsive performance induced PEGylated shell deshielding in tumor extracellular microenvironment and intracellular HPAP degradation, we measure the rapamycin release behavior of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa under the simulated cascaded condition. As presented in Fig. 2F, PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa releases only 5% rapamycin within 48 h at pH 7.4, exhibiting favorable stability without undesired premature release in physiological pH value mimicking blood condition. The restricted release at pH 7.4 is benefited from the two layers encapsulation including PEGylated shielding layer and retained HPAP layer around carbon core, which traps the rapamycin into the double-network. While obvious accelerated release profile of rapamycin (nearly 25%) is found at pH 6.8, it indicated that pH-triggered rupture of outer PEGylated layer led to partial rapamycin release to the tumor extracellular microenvironment, where γδ T cells would likely be activated by partially released rapamycin. Moreover, once the HPAP-CPDs/Rapa are endocytosed into endosome/lysosome and exposed to both acidic pH and high GSH level, the rapamycin release increases remarkably as shown in Fig. 2F. It attributed to the HPAP degradation in the presence of high GSH concentration apart from PEGylated layer rupture. The successive PEG and HPAP layers cleavage contributed to the fastest rapamycin release in the cells, boosting rapamycin-mediated tumor cell inhibition. Hence, PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa exhibited a multi-staged release behavior in blood-tissue-cells cascade, self-adapting to sequential switching physiologic microenvironment.

Taking above results involving of TEM, pH-triggered size shrinkage and charge conversion, GSH-triggered HPAP degradation, endocytosis, lysosomes escape, and drug release into account, PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa exhibited mild acidity-triggered size shrinkage and charge conversion from blood circulation to tumor microenvironment, thereby promoting tumor tissue retention, first-level rapamycin release to activate γδ T cells, cellular uptake, and lysosomes escape. While it also displayed GSH-triggered HPAP degradation after endocytosis, facilitating second-level rapamycin released into cytosol for tumor cells inhibition. Thus, we proposed a platform that integrates multiple properties (size, charge, and degradability) transition into one drug carrier in a blood-tissue-cell cascaded responsive manner to overcome successive biological barriers, meanwhile, this cascaded response synchronizes with staged drug release on demand.

The biocompatibility of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa was further evaluated in terms of hemocompatibility, serum stability, and cytocompatibility. The hemolysis assay, as the commonly used blood compatibility evaluation for assessment of red blood cells (RBCs) membrane integrity [33], was carried out as shown in Fig. 3 A. HPAP-CPDs/Rapa could cause RBCs lysis in a concentration-dependent way and be close to 30% at 200 μg/mL, it was attributed to the electrostatic interaction between positive charged HPAP and negative charged RBC membrane and thereby induced impairment of RBC membrane [34]. Whereas the introduction of PPD shielding layer significantly reduced the hemolysis rate (within 5%), indicating that PPD shell aid to improve the carrier/blood cells interaction. Moreover, similar trend was found in the serum stability assay (Fig. 3B). The size of HPAP-CPDs/Rapa in the presence of serum increased gradually along with the time from 86.5 nm to nearly 600 nm within 180 min, due to non-specific protein adsorption. While PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa could keep its size below 200 nm, benefiting from the PEGylated shell to resist protein adsorption. As to the cell viability experiment (Fig. 3C) with human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs), PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa exhibited no obvious cytotoxicity to HUVECs in the full tested concentration range (0–200 μg/mL) in comparison to HPAP-CPDs/Rapa group that revealed concentration-dependent cell proliferation inhibition when concentration exceeded 50 μg/mL. On the basis of above results, the PPD shielding layer notably improves the blood compatibility during delivery process by eliminating the interaction with blood components.

It was worth noting that not only PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa but also HPAP-CPDs exhibited good cytocompatibility with γδ T cells (Fig. 3D). Generally, positively charged polycation showed cytotoxicity to certain extent. The HPAP-CPDs had no significant toxicity to γδ T cells might ascribed to the intracellular biodegradability of HPAP as similar polymers reported before [35]. In addition, a longstanding barrier in the chemo/immuno combination therapy is attributed to the non-specific cytotoxic effect of traditional chemotherapeutic drugs to immune cells as well, leading chemotherapy-induced immunosuppression. However, our result indicated that either the blank carrier or the rapamycin-containing carrier had no cytotoxic effect on γδ T cells, implying rapamycin-loaded HPAP-CPDs was a potential chemotherapeutic candidate without immunosuppression side effect. Hence, this platform was likely to overcome the current contradiction between immunotherapy and chemotherapy and expected to augment of chemo/immunotherapy synergistic therapeutic effects substantially.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa inhibited proliferation and aggravated apoptosis of tumor cells through down-regulating mTOR signaling in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: In proliferation and differentiation, cells produce proteins, lipids, and nucleotides continuously. The regulation of these processes is closely related to mTOR complex 1 (mTORC1) which controls the balance between catabolism and anabolism in response to environmental conditions[36]. Rapamycin, as a robust mTOR signaling inhibitor, is most effective to block TORC1 activity [37] , [38]. mTORC1 signaling involves receptor-mediated activation of ribosomal S6 kinase, which promotes protein synthesis largely through the phosphorylation of key effectors, p70S6 Kinase (S6K1) (Fig. 4 A). Hence, rapamycin-induced mTOR inhibition is usually determined by the surrogate biomarker of activation-specific phosopho-S6 antibodies.

In an attempt to validate the mildly acidic tumor extracellular microenvironment-triggered selective rapamycin delivery based on successive charge conversion and intracellular HPAP degradation of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, tumor cells were cultured in medium with pH 6.8 or pH 7.4, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4B, rapamycin exhibited obvious proliferation suppression on tumor cells as expected. Other than groups of PBS control, rapamycin and PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa with similar trend at pH 6.8 and pH 7.4, PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa demonstrated a significantly enhanced suppression effect at pH 6.8 than that at pH 7.4, even better than rapamycin group. The superior suppression effect of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa at pH 6.8 might attributed to that PEGylated shell detachment in acidic microenvironment caused charge conversion to expose positively charged HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, which promoted the rapamycin endocytosis and the lysosomes escape, resulting in better tumor cell inhibitory effect even than rapamycin group. While PPD@HPAP-CPDs kept their PEGylated shell without charge conversion at physiological environment pH (7.4) and exhibited no significant inhibitory effect to tumor cell compared with control group. Whereas the PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa without the ability of charge conversion to release rapamycin showed no significant inhibitory effect either pH 7.4 or pH 6.8. Moreover, the results of all groups in apoptosis analysis were consistent with above proliferation trend (Fig. 4C & Figure. S4). Although we found that PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa induced more significant apoptosis than other groups, even higher than Rapa group at pH 6.8, the percentage of apoptosis induced by rapamycin alone and PPD@ HPAP-CDs/Rapa is obviously low due to the main effect of rapamycin is proliferation suppression on tumor cells via mTOR signaling inhibition rather than inducing cell death. In Fig. 4D, western blot was introduced to confirm the phosphorylation of S6 ribosomal protein as the biomarker of mTOR inhibition. As expected, rapamycin, as an inhibitor of mTOR signaling, led to down-regulated expression of p-s6k1. It was worth noting that PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa significantly suppressed the p-s6k1 expression even than rapamycin in medium at pH 6.8, while exhibited negligible effect in medium at pH 7.4, presenting microenvironment-selective mTOR signaling inhibition activity. Other groups also displayed similar trend as proliferation and apoptosis results (Fig. 4D). Taken together, above results indicated that PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa has the capability to exert pH-selective delivery and improved effect on proliferation suppression and apoptosis promotion of tumor cells via inhibiting the mTOR signaling pathway in tumor microenvironment meanwhile avoiding side effect to normal tissues. Benefiting from the microenvironment-driven charge-conversion that promoted endocytosis enhancement and lysosomes escape, PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa exhibited better inhibitory effect on tumor cells than rapamycin alone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa potentiated cytotoxicity of γδ T cells on tumor cells mediated by NKG2D in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: γδ T cells, as early infiltrating immune cells, play critical roles in early anti-tumor immunity and tumor immune surveillance, performing tumor recognition with NKG2D/TCR and tumor killing with cytolytic cytokines [39–42]. STAT5 activation in γδ T cells was reported to promote the expression of NKG2D and TNF-α, which exert the function of tumor cells recognition and elimination [11] , [12]. As reported previously that rapamycin could remarkably suppressed mTOR signaling in γδ T cells but not to induce apoptosis[13], instead the NKG2D and TNF-α expression in γδ T cells was promoted via elevating STAT5 induced by rapamycin-caused mTOR signaling suppression (Fig. 5 A).

To elucidate the role of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa in anti-tumor immunity mediated by γδ T cells at different extracellular microenvironment, the expanded γδ T cells were treated with PBS, PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, rapamycin and PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa for 24 h at pH 6.8 and 7.4 in vitro, respectively, and their was no difference between all groups in apoptosis analysis (Figure. S5). Firstly, tumor cells were co-cultured with γδ T cells that treated with above-mentioned formulations and viable tumor cell number was counted. As shown in Fig. 5B, only rapamycin-treated group showed γδ T cells-mediated tumor cells killing effect at pH 7.4, other groups revealed similar viable tumor cell number as control. While incubated at pH 6.8, PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa-treated group exhibited the best killing effect on tumor cells even than rapamycin group. The pH-selective tumor cells killing effect mediated by γδ T cells was also attributed to the charge conversion-induced positive charge exposure of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa in acidic environment (pH 6.8), promoting the endocytosis and lysosomes escape of rapamycin into γδ T cells that were activated to exert anti-tumor immunity. Whereas the PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa group without charge-conversion ability displayed similar quantity of tumor cells compared with control group either at pH 7.4 or at pH 6.8 owing to the presence of shielding effect of PEGylated shell. Moreover, the enhanced rapamycin uptake that was confirmed in Section 2.2 resulted in the better anti-tumor immunity of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa-treated γδ T cells than rapamycin group after charge conversion at pH 6.8.

NKG2D and TNF-α, the former for recognition and the latter for tumor killing, are key mediators for anti-tumor immunity based on γδ T cell. In this study, the anti-tumor immunity mediated with NKG2D and TNF-α pathway in γδ T cells that treated with various formulations at different pH values was performed. The results of flow cytometry analysis showed in Fig. 5C demonstrated that NKG2D and TNF-α expression were significantly increased after treatment with PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa compared with other three groups at pH 6.8, even tower over rapamycin group for TNF-α. While NKG2D and TNF-α expression was up-regulated only in rapamycin group at pH 7.4. Above results were consistent with γδ T cells-mediated tumor cells killing experiment and verified that PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa could significantly potentiated the anti-tumor immunity of γδ T cells via elevated NKG2D and TNF-α expression at pH 6.8. Western Blot was performed to confirm the effect of STAT5 signaling pathway in the promotion of NKG2D and TNF-α. The result showed that p-STAT5 was increased upon the inhibition of mTOR signaling by rapamycin. PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa-treated group displayed the most remarkable p-stat5 up-regulated effect, compared with other three groups at pH 6.8 (Fig. 5D). It clearly indicated that γδ T cell treated with PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa augmented the expression of NKG2D and TNF-α through STAT5 signaling elevating in acid tumor environment.

On the basis of above results, they converged to support the incentive effect of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa on exerting improved γδ T cell-based anti-tumor immunity mediated by NKG2D via elevating STAT5 signaling, benefiting from cascaded response-mediated rapamycin release with tumor microenvironment selectivity and endocytosis enhancement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The image-tracking of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa and its histocompatibility
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the tumor-targeting ability and imaging-tracking efficacy of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa in vivo, the 4T1 tumor-bearing mouse model was constructed. Considering that CPDs fluorescence was retained and detectable in tumor and organs, we excised the organs and tumors from mouse model and the accumulated fluorescence of CPDs were recorded after intravenous administration of different formulations at day 14th. In Fig. 6 A, mice treated with PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa presented stronger fluorescence signal at the tumor site, compared with other groups treated with PBS and PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa and rapamycin, indicating PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa had a better tumor targeting efficiency. By comparison, negligible fluorescence was detected in main organs. To address the absent fluorescence signal in main organs, we further recorded the ex vivo fluorescence images at 8 h after formulations administration (Figure S6). It was found that florescence signals still existed in liver, spleen and other organs, but weaker than in tumor. It demonstrated that CPDs accumulated in liver, spleen and other organs showed a time-dependent manner and were almost excreted for 2 days except tumor site. Besides, to assess the biosafety of the PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, the hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining of major organs (spleen, heart, liver, kidney and lung) were measured. No obvious tissue denaturation or organ damage appeared in major organs from PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa-treated group in comparison to PBS control (Fig. 6B), implying that PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa has favorable biocompatibility in vivo. Furthermore, we analyze the blood indices of alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate transaminase (AST), blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine (CR)and found there were no significant difference between four groups (Figure. S8). While, the body weight were increased when mice treated with PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa or rapamycin, compared to treated with PBS or PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa (Figure. S7). Above results suggest that PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa displayed an imaging-traceable, tumor-targeted delivery behavior, benefiting from outstanding photoluminescence property of HPAP-CPDs, PEGylated shielding shell-mediated prolonged circulation and tumor accumulation mediated by microenvironment-activated size shrinkage and charge conversion.

As the result of the enhanced rapamycin delivery with tumor extracellular microenvironment selectivity at cellular level, the therapeutic efficacy of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa was further evaluated in vivo by utilizing mice model bearing 4T1 tumors. It was found in Fig. 7 A&B that the PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa was able to significantly suppress the tumor growth, superior to other groups treated with rapamycin and PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa. It was also found that the trend of in vivo tumor inhibitory effect treated with various formulation was consistent with the tumor cells suppression experiments in vitro.

Besides, the curative effects were also confirmed by staining collected tumor tissue with the H&E, Ki67, and terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase dUTP nick end labeling (TUNEL) respectively to assess the histological damage and apoptosis levels on day 14. It was found that the tumor tissue collected from the mice treated with PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa showed severe histological damage, while the mice treated with rapamycin showed less histological damage, and there were no obvious damage on tumor collected from the other two groups treated with PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa and PBS, respectively (Fig. 7C). In addition, the Ki67 staining showed that PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa could significantly suppress the proliferation activity of cells in tumor tissue, while the inhibition effect of rapamycin and PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa groups were much weaker (Fig. 7D). Furthermore, the TUNEL staining assay that visualized apoptotic cells in the xenografts tumor (Fig. 7E) showed consistent trend to in vitro apoptosis results mentioned in Section 2.4 and further supported above Ki67 and H&E staining results. The results of H&E, Ki67, and TUNEL demonstrated the effective tumor suppression efficiency in vivo of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa through inducing apoptosis and proliferation inhibition, which was in line with in vitro aggravated apoptosis and inhibited proliferation of tumor cells mediated with mTOR signaling down-regulation and γδ T cells-induced anti-tumor immunity. Moreover, precise rapamycin delivery involving tumor accumulation and internalization enhancement mediated by microenvironment-driven cascaded responsiveness also contributed to the improved in vivo anti-tumor efficiency of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa.

Besides, to assess the in vivo anti-tumor effect of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa based on γδ T cells, we collected tumor tissue and isolated T cells for flow cytometry analysis. The result revealed that the expression of NKG2D on γδ T cells from PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa-treated mice was higher than that in other three groups, especially higher than rapamycin-treated mice (Fig. 7F), the detail of FACS analysis of γδ T cells from tumor tissue was show in Figure. S9. It attributed to the precise delivery capacity of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa in vivo benefiting from blood-tissue-cells cascaded responsiveness to overcome sequential physiologic barriers. These results indicated that PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa could significantly inhibited in vivo tumor growth via blocking mTOR signaling, as well as promote γδT cell-mediated anti-tumor immunity through NKG2D, thus exerting synergetic chemo/immunotherapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa-potentiated synergy of chemotherapy and immunotherapy to the tumor in TCR-δ KO mice
ce:section.ce:para: To further validate the γδ T cell-mediated anti-tumor immunotherapy effect of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, the TCR-δ KO mice was utilized in PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa-treated and 4T1 tumor-bearing mouse model. TCR-δ KO mice were γδ T cell receptor expression deficient (T-cell receptor delta chain knock-out) in all adult lymphoid and epithelial organs, and was applied to study the role of γδ T cell in the immune response (Fig. 8 A). In this study, four groups of TCR-δ KO mice was designed as follows (Fig. 8B): Ⅰ. control group, TCR-δ KO mice bearing 4T1 tumors was treated with PBS intravenously; Ⅱ. γδ T group, γδ T cells was reconstitution in TCR-δ KO mice which was also generated 4T1 tumor and was then treated with PBS intravenously; Ⅲ. PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa group, TCR-δ KO mice bearing 4T1 tumors was treated with PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa intravenously; Ⅳ. γδ T+PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa group, γδ T cells was reconstitution in TCR-δ KO mice which was also generated 4T1 tumor and was then treated with PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa. It was found that the group with simultaneous γδ T cells reconstitution and PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa treatment displayed the most prominent tumor growth inhibition. As contrast, the group treated only with PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa without γδ T cells reconstitution that performed only chemotherapy exhibited moderate tumor growth inhibition, while the group with only γδ T cells reconstitution showed limited inhibition to tumor growth than control group (Fig. 8C). Furthermore, the tumor images showed the tumor size that was consistent with the trend to those above-mentioned tumor growth results (Fig. 8D). In a word, our data confirmed the presence of γδ T cells-mediated anti-tumor immunotherapy effect and further indicated that PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa facilitated synergistic effect of chemotherapy by virtue of mTOR signaling inhibition and immunotherapy mediated by γδ T cells.

In summary, we have developed a programmable theranostic nanoplatform based on HPAP-decorated carbonized polymer dots (CPDs),which can be prepared easily and with microenvironment-activated blood-tissue-cells cascaded responsiveness, and imaging-trackable capacity for precise rapamycin delivery to exert synergy of chemotherapy and γδ T cell-mediated immunotherapy against tumor. This core/shell structured PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa was endowed with cascaded responsiveness responding to multistage biological barriers including improved blood compatibility, effective tumor accumulation, enhanced endocytosis and lysosomes escape, and staged drug release on demand facilitated by microenvironment-triggered programmable properties (size, charge, and degradability) transition. In vitro results demonstrated that PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa not only promoted chemotherapy inhibitory effect on tumor cells by down-regulating mTOR signaling, but also potentiated γδ T cells-mediated immunotherapy against tumor cells by promoting phosphorylation of STAT5 in a microenvironment-selective manner. Moreover, in vivo results further confirmed the tumor-targeted delivery capability and γδ T cells-assisted anti-tumor efficiency with excellent intravital compatibility. It was worth mentioning that TCR-δ knock-out mice model was employed to validate the PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa facilitated synergistic effect of chemotherapy and γδ T cells-mediated immunotherapy. In addition to integrating multiple nanometer properties into a programmable cascade that adapts to a continuous biological barrier to achieve imaging traceable and precise delivery, we also provide a paradigm that can be demonstrated without chemotherapy-induced immunosuppression, and the synergistic effect of chemotherapy/immunotherapy broaden the field of nanomedicine immunotherapy-assisting γδ T cell-mediated replacement.

1-(2-aminoethyl)-piperazine (AEPZ), cystamine dihydrochloride, citric acid, acryloyl chloride, N‑hydroxy-succinimide (NHS), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)−3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC•HCl), mPEG-OH (2000 Da), 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP), succinic anhydride (SA) and 2,3-dimethylmaleic anhydride (DMMA) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Rapamycin was purchased from Selleck Chemicals (Houston, Texas, USA). Branch polyethyleneimine with molecular weight of 600 Da were purchased from Alfa Aesar (UK). Organic solvents were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (China). Fresh human blood collected in sodium citrate tube was obtained from consenting and healthy volunteers. The red blood cells (RBCs) and plasma were isolated by centrifugating whole blood at 1000 g for 5 min, and the RBCs were washed thrice before use.

Fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin streptomycin and Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) were purchased from Invitrogen Corporation (Washington, USA). p-stat5 and p-S6K1 were purchased from Cell Signaling Technology (Danvers, MA, USA). Biotin-conjugated anti-mouse TCR-δ antibody and purified anti-mouse CD28 mAb were purchased from Sungene Biotech (Tianjin, China)., FITC-conjugated anti-mouse CD3, PE-conjugated anti-mouse TCR-δ, APC-conjugated anti-mouse NKG2D, Purified anti-mouse TCR-δ, and Alexa Fluor 488-conjugated anti-mouse TNF- α were purchased from Biolegend (San Diego, CA, USA). Anti-LAMP1 antibody was purchased from Abcam (Cambridge, MA, USA).

Human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) and 4T1 tumor cells were maintained in DMEM medium with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 5% CO2 and 37°C. As previously described, Splenic γδ T cells were activated by soluble anti-CD28 antibody (1 μg/mL) and purified anti-mouse TCR-δ (10 μg/mL) and expanded in the medium contained with IL-2 (2 ng/mL) for 5 d [43]. After expansion, cells were sorted by immunomagnetic beads with biotin-conjugated anti-TCR-δ antibody and used for following experiments. The purified cells after sorting was verified by flow cytometry.

C57BL/6 mice (6 to 8 weeks of age) were obtained from Jinan Pengyue Experimental Animal Co. LTD (Jinan, China). TCR δ−/-
- / - mice with γδ T-cell receptor deficiency in all lymphoid and epithelial organs were purchased from The Jackson Laboratory (Bar Harbor, ME). All TCR δ−/-
- / - mice were weighed 20–30 g, males and aged 7–8 weeks old when constructing model. Mice were fed in the animal-care facility at Jinan University (Guangzhou, China) with specific pathogen-free conditions. All animal experiments were performed in accordance with regulations of Animal Ethics Committee of Jinan University.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of HPAP-CPDs and their optical performance
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of hyperbranched poly(amido amine) with disulfide bonds contained and polyethyleneimine (PEI, 600 Da) end-capped (HPAP) was performed by one-pot michael addition polymerization with N,N’-bis(acryloyl)cystamine (BAC) and 1-(2-aminoethyl)-piperazine (AEPZ) according to our previous report [24]. Briefly, AEPZ (0.75 mM) was added dropwise into methanol/water mixture (v/v = 3/1, 10 mL) contained with BAC (1.5 mM) and CaCl2 (200 mM) under N2 protection and stirred for 30 h at 50 °C. Thereafter, bPEI600 (0.75 mM) was used to terminate vinyl groups for a further 8 h. Finally, the product was subjected to dialysis (MWCO = 3500) for 2 days. Carbonized polymer dots (HPAP-CPDs) functionalized with HPAP were facilely prepared by one-pot approach through microwave-assisted carbonization process. In brief, citric acid (250 mg) and HPAP (100 mg) were dissolved into 5 mL of HCl solution (0.05 M) in a flask. Then the mixture was subjected to microwave treatment for 5 min. The product was dissolved with water and prufied by dialysis (MWCO = 1000 Da) for 24 h in the dark. The residue solution was concentrated into powder with a vacuum freeze-drying and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

The optical performance of HPAP-CPDs was both characterized by UV-visible spectrum (UV-2550, Shimadzu Corporation, Japan) and fluorescence spectrum (F-7000, Hitachi Corp., Japan). UV-visible absorption spectrum was measured in the range of 240–600 nm, HPAP was employed as a control group. While the fluorescence emission spectra of HPAP-CPDs solution were measured under excitation wavelength in the range of 320–600 nm with the interval of 40 nm. Besides, the fluorescence of HPAP-CPDs in comparison to HPAP was observed directly under a UV Lamp at the wavelength of 365 nm. TEM (JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan) was introduced for morphology observation of HPAP-CPDs. Moreover, cell imaging was performed to assess the bioimaging ability of HPAP-CPDs using laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSM 880, Zeiss, Germany). After seeding 4T1 cells in culture plates for 24 h incubation, we treated cells with HPAP-CPDs (80 μg/mL) for another 3 h. Then, the CPDs-treated 4T1 cells were subjected to fluorescence observation (excitated at 405, 488, and 543 nm) after PBS wash.

Firstly, mPEG2000 OH (10 g) was conjugated with succinic anhydride (2.5 g) in 50 mL of DMF in the presence of DMAP (240 mg) under N2 protection for 24 h at 75 °C, to yield mPEG-COOH after dialysis. Secondly, mPEG-PEI synthesis was performed by a typical amidation reaction in which EDC•HCl (447 mg), NHS (288 mg), mPEG-COOH (100 mg), and bPEI600 (167 mg), were mixed in 25 mL of H2O and reacted for 24 h at room temperature. Finally, dimethylmaleic acid (DMMA) modified mPEG-PEI (PPD) and its control group mPEG-PEI-SA (PPS) were obtained by adding 121 mg of 2,3-dimethylmaleic anhydride or 96 mg of succinic anhydride slowly into the mPEG-PEI (100 mg) solution with the pH value kept in the range of 8–9 for 5 h reaction at room temperature, respectively.

For PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa preparation, 5 mL acetone solution contained with 5 mg rapamycin (Rapa) was added to 15 mL HPAP-CPDs (10 mg) aqueous solution dropwise under vigorous stirring. After 1 h stirring, 10 mg/mL of PPD was added into the above mixture with final mass ratio of PPD/HPAP-CPDs was 2/1 and kept stirring for another 1 h. The precipitated free Rapa was removed by centrifuging for 15 min at 3000 rpm and the supernatant was collected for freeze-drying to obtain PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa. Similar procedure was conducted to prepare PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa. The drug loading capacity and drug release profile at different pH and GSH milieu was determined by HPLC measurement at 277 nm as our previous report [44], in which mobile phase was the mixture of acetonitrile/ultra purewater (9:1, v/v). Besides, the morphology of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa prepared as above shell/core ratio was further observed by HRTEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-triggered charge and size alteration of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa
ce:section.ce:para: The size and charge alteration of formulations (PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, and PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa) were determined by zeta potential analysis and DLS on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS at 25°C. All formulations with the concentration of 100 μg/mL were both measured in the aqueous solution at two pH values (pH 6.8 and 7.4), respectively.

For cellular uptake measurement, 4T1 cells were cultured in BMDM medium (pH 6.8 and pH 7.4) for 24 h, and then treated with PBS, PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, and PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa for 6 h, respectively. The treated cells were collected and then subjected to flow cytometry analysis (FACSAria, BD) with the 488 nm fluorescence channel after washing twice.

As to lysosome escape experiment, 4T1 cells were cultured in BMDM medium (pH 6.8) and treated with PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa for 6 h. Then the treated cells were successively underwent fixation (4% paraformaldehyde), permeabilization (1% Triton X-100 for 1 h at 37 °C) and blockage (5% BSA for 1 h). Subsequently, cells were stained with anti-LAMP1 (Lysosome-Associated Membrane Glycoprotein 1) antibody overnight. After incubation with Alexa Fluor 594-conjugated anti-rabbit IgG for 1 h, the PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa-treated lysosome-tagged cells on the coverslips were imaged with a confocal laser scanning microscope (LSM 880, Zeiss, Germany).

In the hemolysis assay, 1 mL of PPD@HPAP-CDs/Rapa at concentration gradient (10–200 μg/mL) was mixed with 50 μL of RBCs suspended PBS (16% v/v) respectively, HPAP-CDs/ Rapa at the same concentration was set as the control group. After 12 h incubation, the supernatants of above mixed RBC suspensions were collected by centrifugation and transfered into 96-well plate for measurement on a microplate reader (Multiskan MK3, Thermo scientific) at 540 nm. The absorbance ratio of samples (PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa or HPAP-CPDs/Rapa treated RBCs) to that of the complete hemolysis standard (deionized water treated) was calculated as hemolysis rate.

The serum stability of PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa was assessed by measuring the particle size variation of the complex in 10% FBS-contained PBS with HPAP-CPDs/Rapa as the control. The measurement were performed by dynamic light scattering (DLS) for 0, 15, 30, 60, and 180 min at 37 °C.

For cytotoxicity evaluation, HUVECs and γδ T cells with density at 1 × 106 cells/mL were cultured in 96-wells plates and then treated with various formulations (PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, HPAP-CPDs/Rapa and PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa) for various concentration for 24 h. Later, the cell viability was measured by cell counting kit-8 (Dojindo, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell proliferation, apoptosis and western blot
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 tumor cells were treated with various formulations (PBS, PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, rapamycin, and PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa) with rapamycin concentration at 100 nM for 24 h. After seeding 5 × 104 cells in six-well plates and subsequently treating with various formulations at pH 7.4 and 6.8, respectively, cell proliferation was determined by recording cell numbers in 0–4 days after being seeded.

To determine the level of apoptosis following treatment with various formulations (PBS, PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, rapamycin, and PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa), the Annexin-Ⅴ and 7-AAD stain of treated 4T1 tumor cells was performed. The cells were stained with Annexin-Ⅴ for 10 min incubation and 7-AAD for 5 min at room temperature. 20,000 events in total were recorded using a flow cytometer, and the proportion of apoptotic was analyzed.

To confirm the phosphorylation of p70S6 Kinase (S6K1), various formulations (PBS, PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, rapamycin and PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa)-treated 4T1 cells were collected and lysed in lysis buffer (1.5 M NaCl, 1 M HEPES, 1% NP40, 0.1 M Na4P2O7, 0.1 M NaF, 0.1 M Na3VO3, protease inhibitor, phosphatase inhibitors) for 30 min. The protein concentration of the supernatant was measured using a BCA™ protein assay kit after centrifugation for 20 min at 12,000 g under 4°C. Western blot analysis was performed as previously described[45]: proteins were separated by SDS-PAGE gradient gels (12% Bis-Tris) and transferred to polyvinylidence difuoride membranes, followed by incubating with the indicated antibodies (p-s6k1, β-actin). Finally, immunoblotting were determined on a gel imaging system (ChmiDoc MP, Bio-rad).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-tumor immunity mediated by γδ T cells
ce:section.ce:para: γδ T cells were expanded in the presence of IL-2 (2 ng/mL) in vitro and then treated with various formulations (PBS, PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, rapamycin, and PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa) with rapamycin concentration at 100 nM for 24 h. Co-culture of formulations-treated γδ T cells and tumor cells was described as previously [41]. In brief, a total volume of 200 μL mixture composing of γδ T cells (5 × 104 cells/well) (treat with various formulations) and tumor cells (1 × 104 cells/well) was co-cultured in a 96-well plate at pH 7.4 and 6.8, respectively. 24 h later, viable tumor cells were counted using trypan blue.

To assess the expression of TNF-α and NKG2D from γδ T cells after treatment with various formulations, the treated cells were stained for 20 min at 4°C with the indicated combination of fluorochrome-conjugated antibodies (Alexa Fluor 488-conjugated anti-mouse TNF-α and APC-conjugated anti-mouse NKG2D). After staining, the cells were resuspended in 200 μL buffer and subjected to flow cytometry analysis (FACSAria, BD). To further confirm the NKG2D expression by γδ T cells treated with various formulations, phosphorylation of STAT5, as the upstream moderator of NKG2D, was assessed by Western Blot as aforementioned procedure.

To confirm PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa accumulated in the tumor tissue, the 4T1 tumor-bearing mouse (C57BL/6) model was constructed in flank near axilla by subcutaneous injection of 1 × 106 cells/mouse. 100 μL of various formulations (saline, PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, rapamycin, and PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa) were injected intravenously every other day, the dose of rapamycin in each mouse was 1.5 mg/kg. On 14th day, the main organs and tumors were excised from mice for in vivo fluorescence imaging using the IVIS Spectrum imaging system (PerkinElmer, USA).

The 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were administrated with different formulations (saline, PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, rapamycin, and PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa) every other day. On 14th day, the tumor tissues and main organs (kidney, lung, spleen, heart, and liver) were isolated from mouse before fixing with 4% formaldehyde. The slices of organs and tumor tissue were stained with H&E, tunnel kit, and ki67, and examined with the optical microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of cell suspension from tumor tissue and FACS analisis of γδ T cells
ce:section.ce:para: The tumor-bearing mice were treated with four various formulations for 14 days respectively. At day 14, tumor tissues were resected and digested with collagenase/hyaluronidase digestion solution buffer (1 mg/mL collagenase Ⅳ, 10 μg/mL DNase Ⅰ, 0.025% hyaluronidase, 2%FBS in RPMI) for 1 h at 37°C. The digested tumor tissues were filtered through a 40 μm cell strainer and then centrifuge at 500 g for 6mins at 4°C with max acceleration. Remove supernatant and re-suspend the pellet with 14 mL 35% Percoll. Centrifuge at 400 g for 13 min at 4°C with minimum acceleration and deceleration (1, for our Eppendorf centrifuge). Remove supernatant and re-suspend the pellet in RBC Lysis buffer (2 mL/tube) for 10 min, and 12 mL 2% FBS PRIM medium was added in tube. Then the cell suspension was filtered and centrifuged. Washed with 2% FBS RIPM, and do this by centrifuging 400 g, 5 min at 4°C. Remove supernatant and re-suspend the pellet with 10% FBS RIPM (50 ng/mL PMA, 1 μg/mL Ionomycin and 1 μg/mL Golgi-Plug), and incubate the cells for 4–6 h at 37°C, 5% CO2. After 4–6 h, cells was collected and stained with V450 anti-CD45, PE-Cy7 anti-CD3, PerCP anti-TCR δ and APC anti-NKG2D antibodies followed with fixation in 2% formaldehyde in PBS. Cells were then permeabilized and stained with AF 488 anti-TNF-α. Gating was performed on CD 45+ cells, and CD3 T cells was gating from CD 45+ cells, γδ T cells was gating from CD 3+ cells, NKG2D and TNF-α were gating from γδ T cells. Then the percentage of NKG2D and TNF-α cells were reported.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antitumor effect on C57BL/6 mouse model
ce:section.ce:para: To establish the tumor xenograft model, the C57BL/6 mice were subcutaneously injecting with 1 × 106 4T1 cells/mouse in flank near axilla. A total of 36 tumor-bearing mice were randomly grouped as follows: (Ⅰ) saline, (Ⅱ) PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, (Ⅲ) rapamycin, and (Ⅳ) PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa. Every two days, above formulations were intravenous injected into grouped mice with equivalent dose of rapamycin (1.5 mg/kg). The tumor size and survival were measured every other day for 14 days by measuring tumor length (L) and width (W) with a vernier caliper. Tumor volume (V) was calculated by: V = L × W2/2. After 14 d, mice were euthanized and the tumors were harvested to capture a photo.

To evaluate the expression of NKG2D on γδ T cells in vivo, grouped mice treated with the above four formulations (PBS, PPS@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa, rapamycin, and PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa) were euthanized at day 14 to collect tumor tissues. The harvested tumor tissues from every group were digested with collagenase Ⅳ and Dnase Ⅰ at 37°C for 1 h and then filtered through a strainer (40 μm) to collect single cell suspensions containing infiltrating γδ T cells. After that, cells were stained with APC-conjugated anti-mouse NKG2D, PE-conjugated anti-mouse TCR-δ, FITC-conjugated anti-mouse CD3 for 20 mins, and the tagged cells were then fixed with 2% formaldehyde for FACS analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antitumor effect on TCR-δ KO mouse model
ce:section.ce:para: To further verify the γδ T cell-mediated immunotherapy effect induced by PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa in vivo, TCR-δ KO mice model with deficient γδ T-cell receptor expression was employed. Four groups of mice were performed as follow: (1) Control group, PBS was intravenous injected into TCR-δ KO mouse on day 0 and tumor cells were inoculated on day 4, then PBS was intravenous injected every other day and tumor growth was recorded every other day from day 5 until day 18. (2) γδ T group, purified γδ T cells were intravenous injected back to TCR-δ KO mouse on day 0 and the presence of γδ T cells in blood was detected on day 3, while tumor cells were inoculated on day 4, then PBS was intravenous injected every other day and tumor growth was recorded every other day from day 5 until day 18. (3) PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa group, PBS was intravenous injected into TCR-δ KO mouse on day 0 and tumor cells were inoculated on day 4, then PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa (15 mg/kg) was intravenous injected every other day and tumor growth was recorded every other day from day 5 until day 18. (4) PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa group, purified γδ T cells were intravenous injected back to TCR-δ KO mouse on day 0 and the presence of γδ T cells in blood was detected on day 3, while tumor cells were inoculated on day 4, then PPD@HPAP-CPDs/Rapa (15 mg/kg) were intravenous injected every other day and tumor growth was recorded every other day from day 5 until day 18. After 14 d, the mice were euthanized and the tumors were harvested to capture a photo.

All data are presented as means ± standard errors. Statistical significance between groups in figures was compared using a two-tailed t-test. While one-way ANOVA was used to comparing data in multiple groups. All statistical analysis were processed using GraphPad Prism software (version 7.01).

Hua Zhang: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Haijie Zhao: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Yan Huang: Investigation, Software, Formal analysis. Guodong Sun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Yi Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Validation.
